From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Mobile killing squads in Nazi Germany

*Einsatzkommando*

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| German *Einsatzkommando* murder Polish civilians in Leszno, Poland on 21 October 1939 | |
| Formation | 1938 (1938) |
| Membership | Approximately 3,000 |
| Founder | [Reinhard Heydrich](/wiki/Reinhard_Heydrich "Reinhard Heydrich") |

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the [Nazi German](/wiki/Nazi_German "Nazi German") ****Einsatzkommandos**** were a sub-group of the **[Einsatzgruppen](/wiki/Einsatzgruppen "Einsatzgruppen")** (mobile [killing squads](/wiki/Killing_squad "Killing squad")) – up to 3,000 men total – usually composed of 500–1,000 functionaries of the [SS](/wiki/SS "SS") and [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo"), whose mission was to exterminate Jews, Polish intellectuals, [Romani](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people"), and [communists](/wiki/Communist "Communist") in the captured territories often far behind the advancing German front.[[1]](#cite_note-Urban2-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Rossino-2) *Einsatzkommandos*, along with *[Sonderkommandos](/wiki/Schutzstaffel#SS-Sonderkommandos "Schutzstaffel")*, were responsible for the systematic murder of Jews during the aftermath of [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa"), the invasion of the Soviet Union. After the war, several commanders were tried in the [Einsatzgruppen trial](/wiki/Einsatzgruppen_trial "Einsatzgruppen trial"), convicted, and executed.

## Organization of the *Einsatzgruppen*

*Einsatzgruppen* ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *special-ops units*) were paramilitary groups originally formed in 1938 under the direction of [Reinhard Heydrich](/wiki/Reinhard_Heydrich "Reinhard Heydrich") – Chief of the [SD](/wiki/Sicherheitsdienst "Sicherheitsdienst"), and *[Sicherheitspolizei](/wiki/Sicherheitspolizei "Sicherheitspolizei")* (Security Police; SiPo). They were operated by the *[Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel")* (SS).[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012405,_412-3) The first *Einsatzgruppen* of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") were formed in the course of the 1939 [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland"). Then following a Hitler-Himmler directive, the *Einsatzgruppen* were re-formed in anticipation of the 1941 [invasion of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2008598,_618-4) The *Einsatzgruppen* were once again under the control of Reinhard Heydrich as Chief of the [Reich Security Main Office](/wiki/Reich_Security_Main_Office "Reich Security Main Office") (RSHA); and after his assassination, under the control of his successor, [Ernst Kaltenbrunner](/wiki/Ernst_Kaltenbrunner "Ernst Kaltenbrunner").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010185-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2012470,_661-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Einsatzgruppen_SD_issued_and_used_dog-tag.jpg/250px-Einsatzgruppen_SD_issued_and_used_dog-tag.jpg)](/wiki/File:Einsatzgruppen_SD_issued_and_used_dog-tag.jpg)

[Dog tag](/wiki/Dog_tag "Dog tag") of a *Sicherheitspolizei Einsatzkommando* member

Hitler ordered the SD and the Security Police to suppress the threat of native resistance behind the Wehrmacht's fighting front. Heydrich met with General [Eduard Wagner](/wiki/Eduard_Wagner "Eduard Wagner") representing [Wilhelm Keitel](/wiki/Wilhelm_Keitel "Wilhelm Keitel"), who agreed to the activation, commitment, command, and jurisdiction of Security Police and SD units in the Wehrmacht's table of operations and equipment (TOE); in the rear operational areas, the *Einsatzgruppen* were to function in administrative sub-ordination to the field armies in order to effect the tasks assigned them by Heydrich. Their principal task (during the war), according to SS General [Erich von dem Bach](/wiki/Erich_von_dem_Bach "Erich von dem Bach"), at the Nuremberg Trials: "was the annihilation of the Jews, Gypsies, and Soviet political commissars". They were a key component in the implementation of the "Final Solution of the Jewish question" (German: *Die [Endlösung der Judenfrage](/wiki/Endl%C3%B6sung_der_Judenfrage "Endlösung der Judenfrage")*) in the conquered territories. These killing units should be viewed in conjunction with the [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust").

The military commanders knew the task of the *Einsatzgruppen*. The *Einsatzgruppen* depended upon their sponsoring army commander for [billet](/wiki/Billet "Billet"), food, and transportation. Relations between the regular army and the SiPo and the SD were close. *Einsatzgruppen* commanders reported that the understanding by [Wehrmacht](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht") commanders of *Einsatzgruppen* tasks made their operations considerably easier.

For Operation Barbarossa (June 1941), initially four *Einsatzgruppen* were created, each numbering 500–990 men to comprise a total force of 3,000.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELongerich2010185-5) Each unit was attached to an army group: *Einsatzgruppe* A to [Army Group North](/wiki/Army_Group_North "Army Group North"); *Einsatzgruppe* B to [Army Group Center](/wiki/Army_Group_Center "Army Group Center"), *Einsatzgruppe* C to [Army Group South](/wiki/Army_Group_South "Army Group South"), and *Einsatzgruppe* D to the [11th German Army](/wiki/11th_Army_(Wehrmacht) "11th Army (Wehrmacht)"). Led by SD, [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo"), and [Criminal Police (Kripo)](/wiki/Kriminalpolizei "Kriminalpolizei") officers, *Einsatzgruppen* included recruits from the [regular police (Orpo)](/wiki/Ordnungspolizei "Ordnungspolizei"), SD and [Waffen-SS](/wiki/Waffen-SS "Waffen-SS"), augmented by uniformed volunteers from the local auxiliary police force.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrowning199810–12-7) When occasion demanded, [German Army](/wiki/German_Army_(Wehrmacht) "German Army (Wehrmacht)") commanders bolstered the strength of the *Einsatzgruppen* with their own regular-army troops who assisted in rounding up and murdering Jews of their own accord.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELangerbein200331–32-8)

## Earliest *Einsatzgruppen* in occupied Poland

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Selbstschutz_leaders_in_Bydgoszcz.jpg/250px-Selbstschutz_leaders_in_Bydgoszcz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Selbstschutz_leaders_in_Bydgoszcz.jpg)

[Invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland"), from left: [Ludolf von Alvensleben](/wiki/Ludolf_von_Alvensleben "Ludolf von Alvensleben"), Erich Spaarmann and Hans Kölzow, *[Selbstschutz](/wiki/Volksdeutscher_Selbstschutz "Volksdeutscher Selbstschutz")* leaders from the [Valley of Death](/wiki/Valley_of_Death_(Bydgoszcz) "Valley of Death (Bydgoszcz)") in [Bydgoszcz](/wiki/Bydgoszcz "Bydgoszcz") (Bromberg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-380-0069-37%2C_Polen%2C_Verhaftung_von_Juden%2C_SD-M%C3%A4nner.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-380-0069-37%2C_Polen%2C_Verhaftung_von_Juden%2C_SD-M%C3%A4nner.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-380-0069-37,_Polen,_Verhaftung_von_Juden,_SD-M%C3%A4nner.jpg)

SD *Einsatzkommando*, Poland 1939

The first eight *Einsatzgruppen* of World War II were formed in 1939 for the [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland"). They were composed of the [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo"), [Kripo](/wiki/Kripo "Kripo") and [SD](/wiki/Sicherheitsdienst "Sicherheitsdienst") functionaries, and deployed during the classified [Operation Tannenberg](/wiki/Operation_Tannenberg "Operation Tannenberg") (codename for murder of Polish civilians) and the [Intelligenzaktion](/wiki/Intelligenzaktion "Intelligenzaktion") lasting till the spring of 1940; followed by the [German AB-Aktion](/wiki/German_AB-Aktion_in_Poland "German AB-Aktion in Poland") which ended in late 1940. Long before the attack on [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), the Nazis prepared a detailed list identifying more than 61,000 Polish targets by name,[[9]](#cite_note-sbc.org-9) with the help of the German minority living in the [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic"). The list was printed as a 192-page-book called *Sonderfahndungsbuch Polen* ([Special Prosecution Book–Poland](/wiki/Special_Prosecution_Book-Poland "Special Prosecution Book-Poland")), and composed only of names and birthdates. It included politicians, scholars, actors, intelligentsia, doctors, lawyers, nobility, priests, officers and numerous others – as the means at the disposal of the SS paramilitary death squads aided by [Selbstschutz](/wiki/Volksdeutscher_Selbstschutz "Volksdeutscher Selbstschutz") executioners.[[10]](#cite_note-Browning-31/34-10) By the end of 1939 already, they summarily murdered around 50,000 Poles and Jews in the annexed territories, including over 1,000 POWs.[[11]](#cite_note-Piotrowski-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Rhodes-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Bohler-13)[[14]](#cite_note-yadvashem-14)

The SS operational groups were assigned Roman numerals for the first time on 4 September 1939. Before that, their names were derived from the names of their places of origin in the German language.[[15]](#cite_note-ipn.gov.pl-kolebka-15)

1. ***Einsatzgruppe* I** or EG I–Wien (under the command of SS-*Standartenführer* [Bruno Streckenbach](/wiki/Bruno_Streckenbach "Bruno Streckenbach")),[[15]](#cite_note-ipn.gov.pl-kolebka-15) deployed with the [14th Army](/wiki/14th_Army_(Wehrmacht) "14th Army (Wehrmacht)")
   1. *Einsatzkommando* 1/I: SS-*Sturmbannführer* [Ludwig Hahn](/wiki/Ludwig_Hahn "Ludwig Hahn")
   2. *Einsatzkommando* 2/I: SS-*Sturmbannführer* [Bruno Müller](/wiki/Bruno_M%C3%BCller "Bruno Müller")
   3. *Einsatzkommando* 3/I: SS-*Sturmbannführer* Alfred Hasselberg
   4. *Einsatzkommando* 4/I: SS-*Sturmbannführer* [Karl Brunner](/wiki/Karl_Brunner_(SS_general) "Karl Brunner (SS general)")
2. ***Einsatzgruppe* II** or EG II–Oppeln (under SS-*Obersturmbannführer* [Emanuel Schäfer](/wiki/Emanuel_Sch%C3%A4fer "Emanuel Schäfer")),[[15]](#cite_note-ipn.gov.pl-kolebka-15) deployed with the [10th Army](/wiki/10th_Army_(Wehrmacht) "10th Army (Wehrmacht)")
   1. *Einsatzkommando* 1/II: SS-*Obersturmbannführer* Otto Sens
   2. *Einsatzkommando* 2/II: SS-*Sturmbannführer* Karl-Heinz Rux
3. ***Einsatzgruppe* III** or EG III–Breslau (under *SS-Obersturmbannführer und Regierungsrat* Hans Fischer),[[15]](#cite_note-ipn.gov.pl-kolebka-15) deployed with the [8th Army](/wiki/8th_Army_(Wehrmacht) "8th Army (Wehrmacht)")
   1. *Einsatzkommando* 1/III: SS-*Sturmbannführer* Wilhelm Scharpwinkel
   2. *Einsatzkommando* 2/III: SS-*Sturmbannführer* Fritz Liphardt

[Massacres in Piaśnica](/wiki/Massacres_in_Pia%C5%9Bnica "Massacres in Piaśnica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Pia%C5%9Bnica_digging_of_the_graves.jpg/250px-Pia%C5%9Bnica_digging_of_the_graves.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pia%C5%9Bnica_digging_of_the_graves.jpg)

Before execution led by EK–16

Shooting in Piaśnica

1. ***Einsatzgruppe* IV** or EG IV–Dramburg (under SS-*Brigadeführer* [Lothar Beutel](/wiki/Lothar_Beutel "Lothar Beutel"),[[15]](#cite_note-ipn.gov.pl-kolebka-15) replaced by [Josef Albert Meisinger](/wiki/Josef_Albert_Meisinger "Josef Albert Meisinger") in October 1939) deployed with the [4th Army](/wiki/4th_Army_(Wehrmacht) "4th Army (Wehrmacht)") in [Pomorze](/wiki/Pomorze "Pomorze") *(see also EG-*V*)*
   1. *Einsatzkommando* 1/IV: *SS-Sturmbannführer und Regierungsrat* [Helmut Bischoff](/wiki/Helmut_Bischoff "Helmut Bischoff")
   2. *Einsatzkommando* 2/IV: *SS-Sturmbannführer und Regierungsrat* Walter Hammer
2. ***Einsatzgruppe* V** or EG V–Allenstein (under SS-*Standartenfürer* [Ernst Damzog](/wiki/Ernst_Damzog "Ernst Damzog")),[[15]](#cite_note-ipn.gov.pl-kolebka-15) deployed with the [3rd Army](/wiki/3rd_Army_(Wehrmacht) "3rd Army (Wehrmacht)")
   1. *Einsatzkommando* 1/V: *SS-Sturmbannführer und Regierungsrat* Heinz Gräfe
   2. *Einsatzkommando* 2/V: *SS-Sturmbannführer und Regierungsrat* Robert Schefe
   3. *Einsatzkommando* 3/V: *SS-Sturmbannführer und Regierungsrat* Walter Albath
3. ***Einsatzgruppe* VI** (under SS-*Oberführer* [Erich Naumann](/wiki/Erich_Naumann "Erich Naumann")), deployed in [Wielkopolska](/wiki/Wielkopolska "Wielkopolska") area
   1. *Einsatzkommando* 1/VI: SS-*Sturmbannführer* Franz Sommer
   2. *Einsatzkommando* 2/VI: SS-*Sturmbannführer* [Gerhard Flesch](/wiki/Gerhard_Flesch "Gerhard Flesch")
4. ***Einsatzgruppe z. b. V.[[16]](#cite_note-16)*** (under SS-*Obergruppenführer* [Udo von Woyrsch](/wiki/Udo_von_Woyrsch "Udo von Woyrsch") and SS-*Oberfürer* [Otto Rasch](/wiki/Otto_Rasch "Otto Rasch")), deployed in [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia") and [Cieszyn Silesia](/wiki/Cieszyn_Silesia "Cieszyn Silesia")

During that period approximately 12,000 to 16,000 Poles, Jews, Czechs, and Germans were murdered. ## *Einsatzgruppe* A

*Einsatzgruppe* A,[[17]](#cite_note-tables-17) attached to the [Army Group North](/wiki/Army_Group_North "Army Group North"), was formed in [Gumbinnen](/wiki/Gumbinnen "Gumbinnen") in East Prussia on 23 June 1941. Stahlecker – its first commander – deployed the unit toward the Lithuanian border. His group consisted of 340 men from the Waffen-SS, 89 from the Gestapo, 35 from the SD, 133 from the Orpo, and 41 from the [Kripo](/wiki/Kripo "Kripo").[[18]](#cite_note-VBi-18) Soviet troops withdrew from the [Lithuanian temporary capital](/wiki/Temporary_capital_of_Lithuania "Temporary capital of Lithuania") [Kaunas](/wiki/Kaunas "Kaunas") (Kovno) the day before, and the city was taken over by Lithuanians during the [anti-Soviet uprising](/wiki/Lithuanian_1941_independence "Lithuanian 1941 independence"). On 25 June, the *Einsatzgruppe* A entered Kaunas with the forward units of the German army.[[19]](#cite_note-Arad-126-19)

### *Jäger* Report

Main article: [Jäger Report](/wiki/J%C3%A4ger_Report "Jäger Report")

The *Jäger* Report is the most precise surviving chronicle of the activities of one *Einsatzkommando*. It is a tally sheet of the actions of *Einsatzkommando* 3—a running total of their killings of 136,421 Jews (46,403 men, 55,556 women, 34,464 children), 1,064 Communists, 653 persons with mental disabilities, and 134 others, from 2 July to 1 December 1941. A second, major sweep occurred in 1942, before [death camp](/wiki/Death_camp "Death camp") murders replaced *Einsatzkommando* open-pit executions. *Einsatzkommando* 3 operated in the Kovno ([Kaunas](/wiki/Kaunas "Kaunas")) district, west of Vilna ([Vilnius](/wiki/Vilnius "Vilnius")) in contemporary Lithuania. (See also [Rollkommando Hamann](/wiki/Rollkommando_Hamann "Rollkommando Hamann"))

## *Einsatzgruppe* B

The operational command of *Einsatzgruppe* B,[[17]](#cite_note-tables-17) attached to the [Army Group Center](/wiki/Army_Group_Center "Army Group Center"), was established under the command of [Arthur Nebe](/wiki/Arthur_Nebe "Arthur Nebe") a few days after the German attack on the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") in [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa"). *Einsatzgruppe* B departed from the occupied city of [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań") (Posen) on 24 June 1941, with 655 men from the Security Police, Gestapo, Kripo, SD, Waffen-SS and the 2nd Company of Reserve Police Battalion 9.[[2]](#cite_note-Rossino-2) On 30 June 1941 Himmler visited the newly formed [Bezirk Bialystok](/wiki/Bezirk_Bialystok "Bezirk Bialystok") district and pronounced that more forces were needed in the area, due to potential risks of partisan warfare. The chase after the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army")'s rapid retreat left behind a security vacuum, which required urgent deployment of additional personnel.[[2]](#cite_note-Rossino-2)

Scrambling to meet the "new threat", Gestapo headquarters in Zichenau ([Ciechanów](/wiki/Ciechan%C3%B3w "Ciechanów")) formed a lesser known unit called *Kommando SS Zichenau-Schroettersburg*, which departed from the sub-station Schröttersburg ([Płock](/wiki/P%C5%82ock "Płock")) under the command of SS-*Obersturmführer* [Hermann Schaper](/wiki/Hermann_Schaper "Hermann Schaper"), with the mission to murder Jews, communists and the NKVD collaborators across the local villages and towns in the [Bezirk](/wiki/Bezirk_Bialystok "Bezirk Bialystok"). On 3 July additional formation of *Schutzpolizei* arrived in [Białystok](/wiki/Bia%C5%82ystok "Białystok") from the General Government. It was led by SS-*Hauptsturmführer* [Wolfgang Birkner](/wiki/Wolfgang_Birkner "Wolfgang Birkner"), veteran of *Einsatzgruppe* IV from the [Polish Campaign](/wiki/Polish_Campaign "Polish Campaign") of 1939. The relief unit, called *Kommando Bialystok*,[[20]](#cite_note-Szarota-20) was sent in by SS-*Obersturmbannfuhrer* [Eberhard Schöngarth](/wiki/Eberhard_Sch%C3%B6ngarth "Eberhard Schöngarth") on orders from the [Reich Security Main Office](/wiki/Reich_Security_Main_Office "Reich Security Main Office") (RSHA), due to reports of Soviet guerrilla activity in the area with Jews being of course immediately suspected of helping them out. On 10 July 1941, Schaper's unit was split into smaller *Einsatzkommandos* due to requirements of Operation Barbarossa.[[21]](#cite_note-Urban-21)

In addition to mass shootings, *Einsatzgruppe B* engaged in public hangings used as a terror tactic on the local population. An *Einsatzgruppe B* report, dated 9 October 1941, described one such hanging. Due to suspected partisan activity in the area around the settlement of Demidov, all males aged fifteen to fifty-five in Demidov were detained in a camp for screening. The screening produced seventeen people identified as 'partisans' and 'communists'. Thereafter, 400 local residents were assembled to watch the hanging of five members of the group; the rest were shot.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeadland199257–58-22)

On 14 November 1941, Nebe told Berlin that, up until then, 45,000 persons had been eliminated. A further report, dated 15 December 1942, established that the *Einsatzgruppe* B had shot a total of 134,298 people.[[23]](#cite_note-23) After 1943, the mass killings of *Einsatzgruppe* B diminished, and the unit was decommissioned in August 1944.

Around 5 July 1941, Nebe consolidated *[Einsatzgruppe](/wiki/Einsatzgruppe "Einsatzgruppe")* B near Minsk, establishing a headquarters and remaining there for some two months. The *[Gruppenführer](/wiki/Gruppenf%C3%BChrer "Gruppenführer")* determined that *[Sonderkommando](/wiki/Schutzstaffel#SS-Sonderkommandos "Schutzstaffel")* 7a and *Sonderkommando* 7b and the *Vorkommando* Moskau would follow the [Army Group Center](/wiki/Army_Group_Center "Army Group Center"), while *Einsatzkommandos* 8 and 9 clean up to the sides of the spearhead. In compliance, *Einsatzkommando* 8 reached [Bialystok](/wiki/Battle_of_Bia%C5%82ystok-Minsk "Battle of Białystok-Minsk") on 1 July, passed through [Słonim](/wiki/S%C5%82onim "Słonim") and [Baranowicze](/wiki/Baranowicze "Baranowicze"), and began systematic mass killing operations in modern-day southern [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") ([eastern Poland](/wiki/Kresy "Kresy") before World War II).[[21]](#cite_note-Urban-21)

On 5 August, Nebe moved his *Einsatzgruppen* command to [Smolensk](/wiki/Smolensk "Smolensk"), where the *Vorkommando* Moskau was concentrated. On 6 August, *Einsatzkommando* 8 reached [Minsk](/wiki/Battle_of_Bia%C5%82ystok-Minsk "Battle of Białystok-Minsk"), remaining there until 9 September 1941. From Minsk, it reached [Mogilev](/wiki/Mogilev "Mogilev"), which became its general headquarters, and from there *Einsatzkommando* 8 effected successive killings in [Bobruisk](/wiki/Bobruisk "Bobruisk"), [Gomel](/wiki/Gomel "Gomel"), [Roslavl](/wiki/Roslavl "Roslavl"), and [Klintsy](/wiki/Klintsy "Klintsy") systematically attacking the local Jewish communities, and killing the inhabitants.

Meanwhile, *Einsatzkommando* 9 was put to work; they had left Treuburg, in eastern Prussia, and reached Vilna on 2 July. Their main theater of mass killing operations were [Grodno](/wiki/Grodno "Grodno") and [Bielsk-Podlaski](/wiki/Bielsk-Podlaski "Bielsk-Podlaski") (Biala-Podlaska). On 20 July it moved its headquarters to Vitebsk, and then exterminated the citizens of Polotzk, Nevel, Lepel, and Surazh. The command progressed to Vtasma, and from there they killed the communities of Gshatsk and Mozhaisk in the Moscow vicinity. The Soviet counter-offensive forced the *Einsatzkommando* to withdraw to Vitebsk on 21 December 1941. Anticipating the fall of [Moscow](/wiki/Battle_of_Moscow "Battle of Moscow"), the *Vorkommando* Moskau advanced to [Maloyaroslavets](/wiki/Maloyaroslavets "Maloyaroslavets"), earlier captured by the Wehrmacht on 18 October 1941. In practice, *Sonderkommandos* 7a and 7b operated behind the vanguard of the army. The actions were fast, in order to prevent the Jews from escaping the advancing German Army. To the south and east of Smolensk and Minsk, the two *Sonderkommandos* left a wake of dead civilians, from [Velikiye Luki](/wiki/Velikiye_Luki "Velikiye Luki"), [Kalinin](/wiki/Tver "Tver"), [Orsha](/wiki/Orsha "Orsha"), [Gomel](/wiki/Gomel "Gomel"), [Chernigov](/wiki/Chernigov "Chernigov") and [Orel](/wiki/Oryol "Oryol"), to [Kursk](/wiki/Kursk "Kursk").

## *Einsatzgruppe* D

The *Einsatzgruppe* D, as a whole,[[17]](#cite_note-tables-17) was attached to the [11th Army](/wiki/11th_Army_(Wehrmacht) "11th Army (Wehrmacht)"). It was established in June 1941 and operated until March 1943. *Einsatzgruppe* D conducted operations in northern [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), [Cernauti](/wiki/Cernauti "Cernauti"), [Kishinev](/wiki/Kishinev "Kishinev") and across the [Crimea](/wiki/Crimea "Crimea"). In March 1943 it was re-deployed in [Ovruch](/wiki/Ovruch "Ovruch") as an anti-partisan unit called *Kampfgruppe Bierkamp*, named after its new commander Walther Bierkamp. The *Einsatzgruppe* D was responsible for the killing of over 91,728 people.[[25]](#cite_note-holocaustresearchproject-25)

## *Einsatzkommando Tunis*

## *Einsatzkommando Finnland*

Officially the *Einsatzkommando der Sicherheitspolizei und des SD beim AOK Norwegen, Befehlsstelle Finnland*, *[Einsatzkommando Finnland](/wiki/Einsatzkommando_Finnland "Einsatzkommando Finnland")* was a German paramilitary unit active in [northern Finland](/wiki/Lapland_(Finland) "Lapland (Finland)") and [northern Norway](/wiki/Northern_Norway "Northern Norway"). Operating under the Reich Security Main Office, and Finnish [Valpo](/wiki/State_Police_(Finland) "State Police (Finland)") [security police](/wiki/Security_police "Security police"), Einsatzkommando Finnland remained a secret until 2008.

## *Einsatzkommando Italien*

*[Einsatzkommando Italien](/w/index.php?title=Einsatzkommando_Italien&action=edit&redlink=1 "Einsatzkommando Italien (page does not exist)")* was a German paramilitary unit active in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), headed by *[Judenreferent](/wiki/Judenreferent "Judenreferent")* SS-*[Hauptsturmführer](/wiki/Hauptsturmf%C3%BChrer "Hauptsturmführer")* [Theodor Dannecker](/wiki/Theodor_Dannecker "Theodor Dannecker").

## Planned *Einsatzkommando* units

* *Einsatzkommando*-6 – planned for the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and headed by Dr. [Franz Six](/wiki/Franz_Six "Franz Six") (Aborted. Six reassigned to special unit to be activated following the capture of Moscow).
* *[Einsatzkommando Ägypten](/wiki/Einsatzgruppe_Egypt "Einsatzgruppe Egypt")* – planned for Jewish residents in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), including Palestine.
